coredata.dc:title: Photochemical deposition of surface-clean silver nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive colorimetric detection of glutathione
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ju, Jian
Zhang, Ruizhong
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel colorimetric sensor is designed for the sensitive detection of glutathione (GSH) based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Ag nanoparticles supported on the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ag NPs-N-GQDs). The Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites are prepared by a facile one-step photocatalytic strategy and only N-GQDs were used as photocatalytic agents. Therefore, the surface of the obtained Ag NPs is clean without the capping of surfactant. TEM, XRD and XPS characterizations clearly show that Ag NPs-N-GQDs have been successfully prepared. Such non-surfactant capped Ag NPs provide naked catalytic surface with highly catalytic activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB with the presence of H2O2 along with a visual color variation. Since GSH can reduce oxTMB, the UV–vis absorption intensity of oxTMB shows a strong dependence on the concentration of GSH. The novel Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of GSH with a low detection limit of 31nM and a wide detection linear range of 0.1–157.6μM. Furthermore, the novel sensor system shows great potential application for GSH detection in blood serum sample, and easy-to-make analytical approaches in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dots
Silver nanoparticle
Glutathione
Chemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs
Effect of the amount of Ag NPs
Effects of reaction time and temperature
Analytical performance of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 sensing system for GSH detection
Selectivity of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 sensing system for GSH detection
Determination of GSH in human serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the mechanism for colorimetric detection of GSH based on Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system.

HRTEM of the N-GQDs (A) and the corresponding size distribution histogram (B). TEM image of Ag NPs-N-GQDs (C) and the corresponding size distribution histogram (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend and text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) XPS survey spectra of N-GQDs (red line) and Ag NPs-N-GQDs (blue line). (B) High resolution Ag 3d XPS spectrum of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs composite. (C) XRD pattern of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-Ag NPs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB, oxTMB and oxTMB containing GSH in NaAC-HAC solution (pH 4.5). Inset shows the corresponding digital photographs of different solutions.
Effects of different experimental conditions on the absorbance of oxTMB: (A) volume of Ag NPs-N-GQDs, (B) volume of H2O2 and (C) reaction time and temperature.
The time-dependent absorbance of oxTMB at 652nm upon addition of different concentrations of GSH.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the proposed system in the presence of different amounts of GSH. (B) The linear calibration plot for GSH detection based on the catalytic reduction of oxTMB. The Vertical coordinates A/A0, where A0 and A are the absorbance intensities in the absence and presence of GSH. Inset shows the photographs of Ag NPs-N-GQDs in the absence and presence of different amounts of GSH.

Under the optimal conditions, the UV-vis absorption intensities of oxTMB upon addition of different possible coexisting substances in human blood serum, including physiological-level species of 300μM uric acid (UA) and glucose, and 30μM amino acids.
Comparison of detection performances of different sensing systems for GSH analysis.
Detection of GSH in human Serum sample.

head.ce:title: Photochemical deposition of surface-clean silver nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive colorimetric detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel colorimetric sensor is designed for the sensitive detection of glutathione (GSH) based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Ag nanoparticles supported on the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Ag NPs-N-GQDs). The Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites are prepared by a facile one-step photocatalytic strategy and only N-GQDs were used as photocatalytic agents. Therefore, the surface of the obtained Ag NPs is clean without the capping of surfactant. TEM, XRD and XPS characterizations clearly show that Ag NPs-N-GQDs have been successfully prepared. Such non-surfactant capped Ag NPs provide naked catalytic surface with highly catalytic activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB with the presence of H2O2 along with a visual color variation. Since GSH can reduce oxTMB, the UV–vis absorption intensity of oxTMB shows a strong dependence on the concentration of GSH. The novel Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of GSH with a low detection limit of 31nM and a wide detection linear range of 0.1–157.6μM. Furthermore, the novel sensor system shows great potential application for GSH detection in blood serum sample, and easy-to-make analytical approaches in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) plays key roles in many physiological processes and is wildly present in the animal tissues [1–3]. It is well-known that the GSH level has become a key indicator in monitoring some diseases, such as skin disease, blood diseases, and cancer, etc [4–6]. Up to date, there is a growing interest in detecting GSH in blood samples to accurately diagnose some diseases at early stage [7,8]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore an efficient method for the sensitive and selective detection of GSH in human blood sample.

Up to now, many analysis methods have been developed for the detection of GSH in human biological systems, such as fluorometry [9–11], high-performance liquid chromatography [12–14], colorimeter [15–17]. and electrochemical methods [18–20] et al. Among these techniques, colorimetric sensing has attracted more interest in biosensor applications due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and the obvious color change that can be visually observed [15,21–24]. Over the past decade, many kinds of fluorescence probes have been developed for the detection of GSH. For example, Yang and co-workers developed a BODIPY-based fluorescent sensor which can be used for the highly selective fluorescent detection of GSH over cysteine and homocysteine [25]. In recent years, a lot of novel small molecule dyes have also been developed as fluorescence probes for the selective detection of GSH [26–28]. 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is a novel kind of safe chromogen reagent which can be oxidized by OH radicals, resulting in the color change [29,30]. Since the pioneering work using Fe3O4 as peroxidase mimetic for catalyzing the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 [31], various nanomaterials have been received increasing attention as peroxidase mimetics [32–37]. Among of these materials, due to the intrinsic peroxidase-like activities of noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), gold and silver NPs have been used for designing novel colorimetric sensors [24,33,38]. Up to now, most of the metallic NPs-based colorimetric sensors focus on Au NPs [39]. In fact, gold and silver have almost identical bulk lattice constants and based on these similarities relatively inexpensive Ag could be expected to have application in high sensitive and low-cost colorimetric sensors [40].

It should be noted that, the easy oxidation of Ag NPs surface is an intrinsic disadvantage, which will seriously affect its application in real systems [33]. Therefore, it is a challenge to improve the stability of Ag NPs for their analytical applications. Up to now, many approaches have been reported to obtain stable Ag NPs through surface capping with biological molecules, organic small molecules and (or) by depositing on carbon materials [41–43]. However, on the other hand, such surfactant-capped metal NPs always exhibit lowered catalytic performance due to the blocked surface active sites [44]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop non-surfactant capped Ag NPs with highly active surface and high stability. Recently, as a novel kind of green reductant and supporting material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention due to their unique properties with high structural stability [45,46]. Jiang et al. demonstrated that nitrogen-doped GQDs can serve as an effective photocatalyst and a good support for synthesizing Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites [47]. The obtained hybrid nanocomposites exhibited excellent catalytic activities for electrochemical sensing of hydrogen peroxide. Therefore, it is desirable to develop novel strategies for designing highly sensitive chemical sensors based on such non-surfactant capped Ag NPs.

Herein, we develop an Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 colorimetric system for the detection of GSH in blood samples. The Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposite was successfully prepared via a simple photochemical process. During the synthesis, the pre-synthesized nitrogen-doped GQDs were used as both reducing agents and steric stabilizer. The present study demonstrates that the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 colorimetric system can be used to sensitively and selectively detect GSH in human blood serum samples. Such low-cost and high-performance colorimetric sensor has also promising application in clinical diagnosis.

Citric acids (CA), dicyandiamide (DCD) and sodium acetate (NaAc) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., China. AgNO3, 3, 3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), acetic acid (HAC), glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA), glucose and other kinds of amino acids were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All Reagents are of analytical grade and used as received. Nanopure water (18.3MΩcm) was used throughout the experiments.

The morphology and structure of the as-synthesized N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-600) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI TECNAI F20 EM). XPS measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB VG Scientific 250 spectrometer equipped with monochromatized Al Ka excitation. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out by using an X-ray D/max-2200vpc (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) instrument operated at 40kV and 20mA and using CuKα radiation (λ =0.15406nm). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-1700 (MAPADA, China) spectrometer with a 1cm quartz cuvette.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on our previous work [46], N-GQDs were prepared here by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and dicyandiamide (DCD). The Ag NPs-N-GQDs were synthesized according to the method reported previously [47]. Typically, 3mL N-GQDs (20mg/mL) was transferred into 57mL of nanopure water, followed by addition of 0.025g AgNO3. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was strongly stirred in the dark for 30min. The mixture was irradiated under the visible light with λ=420nm for 60min under magnetic stirring. In order to control the reaction temperature, the reactor was placed in a cool water bath. After irradiation, the obtained brown solution was stored in refrigerator. The concentration of the obtained Ag NPs-N-GQDs aqueous solution is 1.2mgmL−1.

A typical colorimetric analysis for GSH was performed as follows. In order to avoid the oxygen dissolved in the solution may oxidize TMB, prior to use, the NaAC buffer solution was first purged with nitrogen for 20min. Stock solutions of GSH (0.01M) and TMB (10mM) were prepared in water and ethanol, respectively. For the detection of GSH, 50μL TMB (0.01M), 100μL Ag NPs-N-GQDs (1.2mgmL−1) and 100μL H2O2 (1.00M) were added to the 2.75mL 0.02M of NaAC buffer solution (pH 4.5) sequentially. The mixture was then heated to 37°C, and held for 15min. Due to the formation of oxTMB, the solution showed bright blue color with a maximum absorbance at 652nm. UV–vis spectra were measured and used for the quantification of GSH and the absorbance at 652nm was used for quantitative analysis.

The morphologies of the as-synthesized N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs were characterized by HRTEM and TEM. Fig. 1 A shows the HRTEM image of the N-GQDs, from which good crystallization can be observed. From the size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 1B, the average diameter of the N-GQDs is 3.8±0.5nm. Fig. 1C shows the TEM image of the produced Ag NPs supported on N-GQDs. One can see that although no any reducing agent and surfactant were used during the synthesis, Ag NPs with a uniform particle size can be produced by a visible light irradiation of the precursors. Based on the size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 1D, the average diameter of the Ag-N-GQDs is about 19±3nm. As a control experiment, under the same experimental condition, the silver nitrate solution (0.025g AgNO3 in 60mL nanopure water) without N-GQDs was irradiated under the visible light with λ =420nm for 60min. As shown in Fig. S1, there is no obvious change of the UV–vis absorption spectra with and without the irradiation of visible light. Such result indicates that the N-GQDs play a key role in the photocatalytic synthesis of Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites.

The compositions of the produced N-GQDs and Ag NPs-N-GQDs were then characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 A shows the survey spectra of N-GQDs (red line) and Ag NPs-N-GQDs (blue line). In both spectra, there are three predominant peaks at 284.0, 400.0, and 530.6eV, corresponding to the C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [4,48]. However, compared to the spectrum of N-GQDs, four additional peaks around 368.2, 374.3, 574.2 and 604.1eV can be observed in the spectrum of Ag NPs-N-GQDs composite, corresponding to the binding energies of Ag 3d, Ag 3p3 and Ag 3p1. From the high resolution XPS of Ag 3d, the two strong peaks at 368.2, 374.3eV can be ascribed to the binding energies of the Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, indicating the metallic state of the formed Ag nanoparticles [47]. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrate that Ag nanoparticles attached on the N-GQDs have been successful produced after visible light irradiation process. The crystalline phase of the as-prepared Ag NPs-N-GQDs was further examined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2C, four obvious diffraction peaks located at 38.0°, 44.2°, 64.3° and 77.3° can be observed, corresponding to the Ag (111), (200), (220) and (311) faces, respectively [49]. In addition, it should be noted that the broad diffraction peak at 2θ ≈25.80° could be assigned to the graphite (002) plane [46]. Above X-ray characterizations clearly demonstrate that Ag NPs supported by N-GQDs complex have been successfully prepared with N-GQDs serving as effective photocatalytic agents. The high stability of graphene quantum-supported metal nanoparticles could be due to the abundant oxygen-rich groups on N-GQDs surface, which can stabilize the deposited silver nanoparticles.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-GQDs before and after the addition of AgNO3 are shown in Fig. 3 A. The initial absorption spectrum of N-GQDs shows two narrow peaks at 230 and 330nm, which could be assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC sp2 domains and the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, respectively [48,50]. After the addition of AgNO3 and the following visible light reduction reaction, the absorption centered at 230nm and 330nm became weaker. Meanwhile, an obvious absorption peak at about 430nm can be observed which is assigned to the surface plasmon absorption of Ag NPs [33]. The oxidase-like activity of Ag NPs in the catalysis of TMB with the presence of H2O2 was evaluated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 3B shows the UV–vis spectra of different solutions. For TMB solution, there is no absorption peak in the visible region. However, when TMB is mixed with Ag NPs-N-GQDs and H2O2, the solution shows two absorption peaks at 652 and 890nm, corresponding to the absorption of the oxidation products of TMB (oxTMB) [32,33]. It should be pointed out that the TMB solution mixed with only H2O2 and N-GQDs exhibits no absorption at 652 and 890nm as shown in Fig. S2. These results clearly indicate that the Ag NPs-N-GQDs can effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into the ·OH radicals, with which TMB can be oxidized to oxTMB [31,34]. From Fig. 3B, it can also be observed that the absorption of oxTMB at 652nm shows decrease upon the addition of GSH. Fig. 3 inset shows the corresponding solution color change [7]. Based on above results, a mechanism was proposed for the detection of GSH, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Upon the addition of biothiols into the oxTMB solution, the formed oxTMB can be reduced to TMB by GSH, resulting in the decrease of absorption peak at 652nm. Therefore, the absorption intensity at 652nm is dependent on the concentration of added GSH. Thus, by monitoring the absorption intensity at 652nm, GSH can be detected sensitively. The related reactions can be described below.
(1)
T M B +
H
2
O
2
→
A g N P s − N − G Q D s
o x T M B +
H
2
O
(2)
o x T M B + G S H → T M B + G S S H + 2
H
+

The catalytic activity of Ag NPs-N-GQD depends on some key factors, such as the amount of Ag NPs, concentration of H2O2, and reaction temperature and time etc. In this work, the absorbance of oxTMB at 652nm was used as the criterion to optimize the detection conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the effect of the volume of Ag NPs-N-GQD on the absorption intensity of oxTMB, different volumes of Ag NPs-N-GQD (1.2mgmL−1) was added into the solution containing 100μL TMB (0.01M) and 100μL H2O2 (1.00M). It can be seen from Fig. 4 A that, the absorption intensity of oxTMB increases with the increase of volume of Ag NPs-N-GQD. Based on the results, in order to control the UV–vis absorption intensity less than 1.0, 100μL of Ag NPs-N-GQD was chose in the following experiments.

The influence of H2O2 concentration on the absorption of oxTMB was examined. As shown in Fig. 4B, a maximum absorption can be obtained when 100μL of H2O2 (1.00M) was used. Therefore, 100μL TMB (0.01M), 100μL of Ag NPs-N-GQD (1.2mgmL−1) and 100μL H2O2 (1.00M) were chose for the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction time and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: In order to shorten the reaction time and quickly reach the steady state of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-H2O2 system, other crucial factors of temperature and reaction time were also examined. Fig. 4C displays that the absorption of oxTMB increases with the increase of temperature in the range from 30 to 40°C; however, further increase of temperature above 37°C results in obvious decreased absorption intensity. Meanwhile, at all the studied temperatures, the absorption intensities of oxTMB can reach steady levels after 15min. Based on these results, the as-mixed solution was maintained at 37°C in water bath for 15min for the following detection experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 sensing system for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: Before the sensitivity study, the time-dependent absorbance of the oxidized TMB at 652nm upon the addition of different concentrations of GSH was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , the UV–vis absorption intensity exhibits almost no change after the addition of low concentration (0–6μM) of GSH for 10min. Moreover, with the concentration of GSH increased to 20μM, the UV–vis absorption at 652nm only decreased about 5% of the initial absorption intensity. This result indicates that short time is required to complete the reaction between GSH and oxTMB, and the sensing system shows a highly operational stability.

Under the optimal conditions, the UV–vis absorption intensity at 652nm was used to measure the concentration of GSH. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the UV–vis absorption intensity of oxTMB decreases with GSH concentration increasing. Fig. 6B shows the dependence of A/A 0 on the concentration of GSH. Two linear calibration curves could be obtained within two concentration ranges. The standard regression equations are A/A 0 =−0.062 CGSH +0.992 (R 2 =0.994) (concentration range: 0.1–1.6μM) and A/A 0 =−0.0052 CGSH +0.866 (R 2 =0.993) (concentration range: 1.6–157.6μM), respectively. The detection limit of GSH with this sensing system is estimated to be 31nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Table 1 summarizes the detection performances of different sensing systems for GSH analysis. It can be seen that the present method exhibits much lower detection limit and wider linear range for GSH detection than most of the previously reported analytical approaches, such as fluorescence and colorimetric methods [2,7,16,38,51–53]. Such result indicates that the present sensing strategy and the Ag NPs-N-GQDs sensing system are promising for the sensitive detection of GSH. Moreover, Fig. 6B inset shows the solution color with different concentrations of GSH. Obvious color change from bright blue to colorless can be observed obviously with increasing the concentration of GSH from 0 to 150μM. Therefore, such a novel sensing system shows great potential application for designing commercial GSH detection assay kit due to the visual color variation for different oxidation degree of oxTMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 sensing system for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of the proposed sensor for GSH detection, under the optimal conditions, the responses of some possible coexisting substances in human blood serum were evaluated, including physiological-level species of 300μM uric acid (UA) and glucose, and 30μM amino acids, such as lysine tryptophan (Trp), isoleucine (lle), threonine (Thr), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Tyr), arginine (Arg), asparagines (Asn), valine (Val), serine (Ser), methionine (Met), glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln), histidine (His), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), aspartic acid (Asn), lycine (Lys), Homocysteine (Hcy) and Cysteine (Cys). It can be seen from Fig. 7 that except for Hcy, Cys and GSH, other coexisting substances in human blood serum exhibit no apparent effects on the absorption intensity of oxTMB. The presence of Hcy, Cys and GSH could lead to the decrease of UV–vis absorption intensity of oxTMB due to the thiol groups of these biothiols can reduce oxTMB efficiently. From Fig. 7, the reduction ability of the biothiols for oxTMB follows the order of GSH>Cys>Hcy. Such result is consistent with the previous reports [7,54]. The different reduction activities of the biothiols may be attributed to the different reducing groups contained in the studied molecules, such as CO and OH, NH, NH2 and -SH functional groups. Therefore, the proposed colorimetric method for the detection of GSH exhibited a good selectivity, and thus it has promising application in physiological sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: With the achieved high sensitivity and good selectivity, the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system was used to detect GSH in blood serum samples. In this work, the standard titration method was carried out to detect GSH in the physiological environment. After the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system reaches stability, certain concentrations of N2-saturated clinical blood serum sample was added, and then the standard concentration of GSH was spiked into the system. The detection results are shown in Table 2 . The concentrations of GSH in serum samples were calculated using the calibration curve shown in Fig. 6B and the corresponding standard regression equation. It can be seen that the present sensing system exhibits a high sensing sensitivity for GSH in serum samples. Such results show that the Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system could be used for the sensitive detection of GSH in blood serum samples.

In summary, Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites were prepared by a facile one-step photocatalytic strategy by using N-GQDs as photocatalyst agents without addition of any reductant. The prepared surfactant-free Ag NPs-N-GQDs nanocomposites provide naked catalytic surface with excellent oxidase-like catalytic activities, which can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with the presence of H2O2. Such Ag NPs-N-GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system has been further used for colorimetric detection of GSH sensitively and selectively with a low detection limit of 31nM. The present study provides a simple, low-cost route toward green production of Ag NPs-N-GQDs for sensing GSH in blood serum sample. Furthermore, the novel sensor system shows great potential application for designing commercial GSH detection assay kit due to the visual color variation for different oxidation degree of oxTMB.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575134, 21275136).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.01.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jian Ju earned his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Northeast Normal University, China in 2013. He was a post-doctoral fellow at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests include design, synthesis of metal and carbon nanomaterials and their applications in chemosensors, biosensors and electrocatalysis.

Ruizhong Zhang is currently pursuing her Ph.D. under the direction of Professor Wei Chen at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research interests mainly focus on the design and synthesis of carbonous and noble metal-free nanomaterials, as well as their applications in fuel cells and sensors.

Wei Chen received his Ph.D. in electrochemistry from Xiamen University in 2003. Following his graduate studies, he began working as a postdoctoral associate in the area of synthesis and the property studies of metal nanoclusters at University of California-Santa Cruz. He is currently a full professor at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests include electroanalytical chemistry, electrocatalysis, photoelectrocatalysis, the controlled synthesis, characterization, and application of nanomaterials, especially metal nanoclusters, in fuel cells, solar cells and optical devices.
